content: Control flow determines which statements execute and in what order. JavaScript provides conditional statements to branch on a condition, loop statements to repeat blocks of code, and error-handling statements to manage failures. Angular's template syntax mirrors these constructs with
, making it essential to understand the underlying JavaScript semantics.

content.text: switch vs if-else — When to Use Which
Matching one value against many constants
Conditions involve ranges or complex expressions
All branches share the same expression
TypeScript discriminated unions (use switch for exhaustiveness)

content: loop has three parts: initialiser, condition, and update. Use it when you need the index, need to iterate backwards, or want to skip items by incrementing by more than 1.
— arrays, strings, Maps, Sets, NodeLists, and generator results. It is the preferred loop for most array operations when the index is not needed.
(keys) of an object, including inherited ones. Prefer
on arrays — it iterates array indices as strings and may include prototype properties. Use
when the number of iterations is unknown — polling, parsing streams, or retrying until success.
always executes the body at least once, then checks the condition. Useful for prompts and menus where you need at least one execution before testing.

content.text: Angular's template control flow syntax (introduced in Angular 17) mirrors JavaScript constructs directly. Understanding the JS semantics helps predict Angular template behaviour.

Array Higher-Order Methods as Declarative Control Flow

In modern JavaScript and Angular code, imperative loops are often replaced with array methods that read more declaratively and work well with immutability requirements. These are not technically control flow statements, but they serve the same purpose.
reduce — Accumulate to a Single Value
Side effects per item, no transform needed
Control flow in JavaScript extends to asynchronous code. Angular relies heavily on async patterns — services, HTTP calls, and event streams all involve async control flow.
Early Exit Patterns in Angular Services
